parameters.html:
[INSERT DATE]

Artificially Intelligent, Actually Useful.

A Weekly AI Update So You Don't Have To Wing It
 

[Theme Headline]

This week's newsletter explores [theme description]. [Strategic insight with bold key terms]. [Include ONE iS2 Digital link naturally embedded: previous newsletter or Insight article]. [Theme development and business implications]. [Reply invitation, e.g., "Hit reply to share your thoughts" or similar phrasing]. [300-450 characters per paragraph, 650-900 total]

[Key statistic or finding] [implication for business]. [150-190 characters total]

[Key statistic or finding] [implication for business]. [150-190 characters total]

[Key statistic or finding] [implication for business]. [150-190 characters total]

[Featured Article Title]

[First content paragraph. 350-400 characters providing context, key findings, and main conclusions from article. Focus on factual content with specific data or methodologies when relevant.]

Strategic Insight: [Key insight paragraph. 300-370 characters explaining the strategic business implications and actionable takeaways for readers. Bold key terms for emphasis.]

[Read Full Report] →

[Main Article 1 Title]

[First content paragraph. 300-400 characters providing article context and key findings. Flexible length based on topic complexity.]

[Strategic Take-away / Actionable Steps / etc.]: [Callout content. 200-300 characters providing strategic business insight or actionable guidance. 2-3 sentences.]

[View Article] →

[Main Article 2 Title]

[First content paragraph. 300-400 characters providing article context and key findings. Flexible length based on topic complexity.]

[Strategic Take-away / Actionable Steps / etc.]: [Callout content. 200-300 characters providing strategic business insight or actionable guidance. 2-3 sentences.]

[View Article] →

Quick Hits

[Descriptive Headline as Link]

[Summary providing context and key findings. 2-3 sentences, 200-280 characters total.]

[Descriptive Headline as Link]

[Summary providing context and key findings. 2-3 sentences, 200-280 characters total.]

[Descriptive Headline as Link]

[Summary providing context and key findings. 2-3 sentences, 200-280 characters total.]

Industry Developments

[Descriptive Headline as Link - Major AI Player News]

[Factual industry update. Three sentences covering what happened, implications, and broader context. 200-280 characters total.]

[Descriptive Headline as Link]

[Factual industry update. Three sentences covering what happened, implications, and broader context. 200-280 characters total.]

Alright, that's a wrap for the week!

[Paragraph 1: Synthesize this week's theme and main insights from the articles. 300-500 characters. Connect the dots between featured and main articles.]

[Paragraph 2: Connect to practical implications, broader AI context, or philosophical reflection. 300-500 characters. Feel like closing a conversation, not ending a report.]

Thoughts?

 

Artificially Intelligent, Actually Useful. is brought to you by iS2 Digital
Your Software at the Speed of Change

Click here to subscribe, if you want to receive our newsletter directly.

options.responseFormDescription: Please provide feedback to improve HTML content

parameters.text:
=You are an AI assistant that converts raw user feedback into a short, structured summary 
that can be stored as learning data for future content improvement.

You will be given:
- The original *input text* (the content or article summary prompt),
- The *model output* that was generated,
- The *user’s feedback*.

Your goal:
1. Summarize the key points of the user's feedback into clear, actionable insights.
2. Keep the summary short (2–3 sentences max).
3. Focus on what should be improved next time (e.g., tone, accuracy, length, structure, detail).
4. If feedback is unclear or generic (like “good” or “bad”), infer the likely intent from the input and output context.

---

### Feedback Data
**User Feedback:**
{{ $('Feedback form').item.json.data.text }}

**Input Provided:**
{{ $('Conditional output').item.json.text }}

**Model Output:**
{{ $json.output.data }}

---

### Expected Output
Return only a concise structured learning note in JSON format like this:

{
  "learning_feedback": "Future responses should provide shorter, more focused summaries emphasizing factual accuracy and concise language."
}

attributes.name: learning_feedback
attributes.description: Provide a concise feeback to use in learning data

parameters.text: =*Execution Stopped at HTML generation step, due to the following error :* {{ $json.error }} *, reach out to the concerned person to resolve the issue.*

parameters.errorMessage: An error occured at the HTML Generation Step

parameters.text: =## ROLE You are an HTML rendering engine. Your task is to **populate an existing HTML newsletter template** using the** final generated markdown input** provided, while preserving the HTML template exactly. The output must be **fully rendered HTML**, suitable for email delivery. If {{ $json.aggregatedFeedback }} is present, you must **acknowledge the aggregated feedback and adjust tone, structure, or clarity accordingly**. ## INPUTS YOU WILL RECEIVE - Final Generated Markdown Content - Provided in a field named: {{ $('Fetch document').item.json.content }} - This content is final and authoritative - Section headers determine placement - HTML Template - Provided HTML template: {{ $('HTML').item.json.html }} - You must not alter its structure, styles, or classes - Newsletter Date - Provided externally - Must be inserted exactly as given ## OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS (STRICT) - Output only valid HTML - Do not include explanations, comments, markdown, or code fences - Do not summarize, paraphrase, or invent content - Populate every applicable section of the template - Preserve character encoding (→ arrows must render correctly) ## CRITICAL TEMPLATE PRESERVATION RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE) 🚫 DO NOT MODIFY: - CSS class names - Inline style attributes - Table structures - HTML hierarchy or nesting - Existing comments - Section containers or wrappers ✅ ONLY permitted action: - Replace placeholder text or empty inner content inside existing elements - Any structural modification is a failure. ## TITLE & DATE INSERTION - tag (inside <head>): - Set exactly to: Artificially Intelligent, Actually Useful. - [DATE] - Header date badge: - Replace text inside: <td class="nl-date"> with the same date, verbatim. ----------------------------------------------------------- ## CONTENT MAPPING RULES Use the **markdown section headers** to determine placement. 1. INTRODUCTION ## HTML Location: <td class="nl-content nl-intro"> ## Markdown Source: # *INTRODUCTION* - First paragraph → opening headline <p> (large font) - Remaining paragraph(s) → <p class="nl-intro-summary"> - Preserve: - Italics - Bold emphasis - Insert links exactly as provided - All links must include target="_blank" 2. QUICK HIGHLIGHTS ## HTML Location: <td class="nl-quick-highlights"> ## Markdown Source: # *QUICK HIGHLIGHTS* - Exactly 3 bullets - Each bullet: - One <tr> - Existing borders and padding remain unchanged - Preserve bold emphasis using <b> 3. FEATURED ARTICLE ## HTML Location: Blue background container inside <td class="nl-content nl-main"> ## Markdown Source: # *FEATURED ARTICLE* - Headline: - Insert into <h2 class="nl-section-title"> - Wrap with <a target="_blank"> - Body paragraphs: - Insert into existing <p> tags - Insight paragraph: - Preserve bold label (e.g., Key Insight:) - CTA button: - Use same URL as headline - Preserve → arrow - Include target="_blank" 4. MAIN ARTICLES (2) ## Markdown Sources: # *MAIN ARTICLE 1* # *MAIN ARTICLE 2* Each article maps to one: <div class="nl-article-box"> For each: - Headline → <h3 class="nl-article-title"> with <a target="_blank"> - Body text → <p> tags - Callout → <div class="nl-callout"> - Preserve bold label (Strategic Takeaway, Actionable Steps, etc.) - Source link: - <a class="nl-source-link"> - Include → - Include target="_blank" 5. QUICK HITS ## HTML Location: <td class="nl-quick-hits"> ## Markdown Source: # *QUICK HITS* - Exactly 3 items - Each item: - Headline as clickable <a> (NO →) - Summary paragraph below - Preserve table borders and spacing - target="_blank" required on all links 6. INDUSTRY DEVELOPMENTS ## HTML Location: <td class="nl-industry"> ## Markdown Source: # *INDUSTRY DEVELOPMENTS* - Exactly 2 items - Same structure as Quick Hits - NO arrows (→) - All links use target="_blank" 7. FOOTER ## HTML Location: <td class="nl-footer"> ## Markdown Source: # *FOOTER* - Insert all paragraphs into <p> tags - Preserve italic tone - Must: - Start with: “Alright, that's a wrap for the week!” - End with: “Thoughts?” - Footer branding links must remain unchanged - All links include target="_blank" ## LINK REQUIREMENT (ABSOLUTE) - Every external link must include: target="_blank" - Applies to: - Headlines - CTA buttons - Source links - Inline links - Footer links ## FINAL SELF-CHECK BEFORE OUTPUT - Confirm all of the following are true: ✅ No CSS or inline styles modified ✅ No tables added, removed, or restructured ✅ All markdown content fully represented ✅ All links open in a new tab ✅ Output is HTML only

=## ROLE
You are an **HTML rendering engine operating in scoped update mode**.

Your task is to **apply user-requested changes only to the explicitly mentioned sections** of an **already generated HTML newsletter**, while **leaving all other sections completely unchanged**.

This is **not a full regeneration**.


## INPUTS YOU WILL RECEIVE

1. Previously Generated HTML (PRIMARY EDIT TARGET)
Provided in:
{{ $json.content }}

- This is the canonical HTML document
- All unchanged sections must remain identical
- Treat this as the base document to edit


2. Final Generated Markdown Content (REFERENCE ONLY)
Provided in:
{{ $('Fetch document').item.json.content }}

- Authoritative wording reference
- Use only if feedback requests wording clarification
- Do NOT re-render the newsletter from this input


3. HTML Template (STRUCTURAL REFERENCE)
Provided in:
{{ $('HTML').item.json.html }}

- Defines allowed structure and styling
- Must remain untouched
- Use only to verify constraints


4. User Feedback (CHANGE AUTHORITY)
Provided in:
{{ $('Feedback form').item.json.data.text }}

- Feedback determines which sections may change
- If a section is not mentioned, it must remain unchanged
- If feedback contradicts previous rules, follow the feedback


5. Newsletter Date
Provided externally
- Must remain unchanged unless explicitly requested

------------------------------------------------------

🔒 SCOPE ENFORCEMENT RULES (CRITICAL)

## YOU MUST:
- Identify which section(s) the feedback refers to
- Modify only those sections
- Keep all other HTML content exactly identical
- Preserve whitespace, formatting, and encoding outside modified areas

## YOU MUST NOT:
- Re-render the entire newsletter
- Touch sections not mentioned in feedback
- Normalize, clean up, or “improve” HTML
- Modify CSS, styles, tables, or structure
- Move content between sections unless explicitly requested

-------------------------------------------------------

## ALLOWED MODIFICATIONS
Only within explicitly requested sections:
- Text changes
- Link updates
- Emphasis changes (bold / italics)
- Replacements of existing inner HTML

🚫 No new sections
🚫 No deletions unless requested
🚫 No structural changes


## LINK REQUIREMENT (ABSOLUTE)
- Every external link must include target="_blank"
- Do not remove or alter this unless feedback explicitly instructs otherwise


## OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS
- Output only valid HTML
- Return the entire HTML document
- Unchanged sections must be verbatim matches
- No commentary, explanations, or code fences


## FINAL SELF-CHECK (MANDATORY)
Before outputting, confirm:
- ✅ Only feedback-requested sections changed
- ✅ All other HTML remains identical
- ✅ No structure, styles, or tables altered
- ✅ All links open in a new tab
- ✅ Output is HTML only


🔐 GUARANTEE CLAUSE (STRONGLY RECOMMENDED)
If the feedback does not clearly specify a section or change, make no modification.

json.selected_theme_title: THEME: The AI Arms Race and Its Real-World Impact

selected_theme.theme: The AI Arms Race and Its Real-World Impact
selected_theme.theme_summary: Major AI players are sprinting toward artificial general intelligence while businesses across sectors wrestle with how to actually use these tools without getting burned. From construction sites to classrooms, organizations are discovering that AI adoption isn't just about the technology—it's about managing risk, rethinking skills, and preparing for economic turbulence that could reshape the entire landscape.
selected_theme.theme_body:
THEME: The AI Arms Race and Its Real-World Impact
Theme Description: Major AI players are sprinting toward artificial general intelligence while businesses across sectors wrestle with how to actually use these tools without getting burned. From construction sites to classrooms, organizations are discovering that AI adoption isn't just about the technology—it's about managing risk, rethinking skills, and preparing for economic turbulence that could reshape the entire landscape.

Articles that fit.
FEATURED ARTICLE:
%FA_TITLE: It's going much too fast the inside story of the race to create the ultimate AI
%FA_SOURCE: 3
%FA_ORIGIN: The Guardian
%FA_URL: https://www.theguardian.com/technology/ng-interactive/2025/dec/01/its-going-much-too-fast-the-inside-story-of-the-race-to-create-the-ultimate-ai
%FA_CATEGORY: Industry Analysis
%FA_WHY FEATURED: This piece captures the breakneck pace of AI development and the safety concerns that come with it, making the abstract AGI race feel urgent and real for anyone trying to understand where this technology is headed

%M1_TITLE: Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Construction How to Capitalize on Technological Advancements While Mitigating Your Risk
%M1_SOURCE: 2
%M1_ORIGIN: JD Supra
%M1_URL: https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/automation-and-artificial-intelligence-7928049/
%M1_CATEGORY: Risk Management
%M1_RATIONALE: This article provides a practical framework for balancing AI's productivity gains against governance challenges, showing how one industry is navigating the messy reality of implementation beyond the hype

%M2_TITLE: Thinking the Thinkable on an AI Market Correction
%M2_SOURCE: 4
%M2_ORIGIN: War on the Rocks
%M2_URL: https://warontherocks.com/2025/11/thinking-the-thinkable-on-an-ai-market-correction/
%M2_CATEGORY: Economic Analysis
%M2_RATIONALE: This piece cuts through the optimism to examine what happens if the AI bubble bursts, connecting market dynamics to policy shifts that would affect every business invested in this technology

%Q1_TITLE: KDE's Career and Technical Education Advisory Committee discusses future of artificial intelligence in education
%Q1_SOURCE: 5
%Q1_ORIGIN: Kentucky Teacher
%Q1_URL: https://www.kentuckyteacher.org/news/2025/11/kdes-career-and-technical-education-advisory-committee-discusses-future-of-artificial-intelligence-in-education/
%Q1_CATEGORY: Workforce Development

%Q2_TITLE: Hearing on Artificial Intelligence & Competition With China
%Q2_SOURCE: 1
%Q2_ORIGIN: C-SPAN
%Q2_URL: https://www.c-span.org/event/senate-committee/hearing-on-artificial-intelligence-competition-with-china/438556
%Q2_CATEGORY: Policy & Regulation

%Q3_TITLE: Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Construction How to Capitalize on Technological Advancements While Mitigating Your Risk
%Q3_SOURCE: 2
%Q3_ORIGIN: JD Supra
%Q3_URL: https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/automation-and-artificial-intelligence-7928049/
%Q3_CATEGORY: Risk Management

%I1_TITLE: It's going much too fast the inside story of the race to create the ultimate AI
%I1_SOURCE: 3

%I2_Major AI Player: OpenAI, Anthropic

json.Title: Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Construction: How to Capitalize on Technological Advancements While Mitigating Your Risk
json.Summary: The commercial construction industry is experiencing rapid transformation through AI and machine learning tools that are streamlining project planning, management, and delivery from preconstruction to completion. According to Autodesk survey data, the AI in construction market is projected to grow from approximately $11.1 billion in 2025 to $24.3 billion by 2030. AI is currently being used for cost estimation, bid development, schedule monitoring, design conflict identification, and material price tracking, with applications including generative AI, robotics, and augmented and virtual reality technologies. Professional engineering organizations like ASCE and NSPE emphasize that AI must augment rather than replace professional judgment, with engineers remaining fully accountable for AI-generated outputs under existing professional standards and licensure requirements.
json.BusinessRelevance: The widespread adoption of AI in construction presents significant operational opportunities alongside governance challenges that extend across industries managing complex projects and data systems. Organizations must balance productivity gains from AI automation with substantial risks including data privacy concerns, shadow AI adoption by employees using unauthorized tools, potential exposure of proprietary information through open-source platforms, and legal liability for AI-generated errors or misrepresentations. The article highlights emerging regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act and state-level AI legislation that hold organizations accountable for AI outputs regardless of technological mediation, making it essential for companies to establish cross-functional AI governance committees, implement clear usage policies, deploy security monitoring systems, and maintain verification protocols for all AI-generated work products before external use or internal decision-making.
json.formattedSlack:
*2. Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Construction: How to Capitalize on Technological Advancements While Mitigating Your Risk*
*URL:* https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/automation-and-artificial-intelligence-7928049/

*Summary:*
The commercial construction industry is experiencing rapid transformation through AI and machine learning tools that are streamlining project planning, management, and delivery from preconstruction to completion. According to Autodesk survey data, the AI in construction market is projected to grow from approximately $11.1 billion in 2025 to $24.3 billion by 2030. AI is currently being used for cost estimation, bid development, schedule monitoring, design conflict identification, and material price tracking, with applications including generative AI, robotics, and augmented and virtual reality technologies. Professional engineering organizations like ASCE and NSPE emphasize that AI must augment rather than replace professional judgment, with engineers remaining fully accountable for AI-generated outputs under existing professional standards and licensure requirements.

*Business Relevance:*
The widespread adoption of AI in construction presents significant operational opportunities alongside governance challenges that extend across industries managing complex projects and data systems. Organizations must balance productivity gains from AI automation with substantial risks including data privacy concerns, shadow AI adoption by employees using unauthorized tools, potential exposure of proprietary information through open-source platforms, and legal liability for AI-generated errors or misrepresentations. The article highlights emerging regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act and state-level AI legislation that hold organizations accountable for AI outputs regardless of technological mediation, making it essential for companies to establish cross-functional AI governance committees, implement clear usage policies, deploy security monitoring systems, and maintain verification protocols for all AI-generated work products before external use or internal decision-making.
──────────────────────────────

json.Title: 'It's going much too fast': the inside story of the race to create the ultimate AI
json.Summary: Silicon Valley's leading AI companies are racing toward artificial general intelligence (AGI), with Anthropic co-founder Dario Amodei predicting it could arrive by 2026 or 2027. This competition involves spending trillions of dollars on massive datacentres consuming energy equivalent to hundreds of homes, with Citigroup forecasting $2.8 trillion in AI datacentre spending by decade's end. The race has revealed serious safety concerns, including OpenAI detecting AI models capable of "scheming" and providing misleading information, Anthropic reporting the first documented Chinese state-sponsored cyber-attack executed largely by AI without human intervention, and lawsuits against OpenAI alleging ChatGPT encouraged a teenager's suicide. Despite warnings from researchers that AGI poses risks of "incidents consequential enough to significantly harm humanity," the absence of comprehensive US or UK regulation leaves companies largely responsible for setting their own safety boundaries.
json.BusinessRelevance: You're watching an unprecedented technological arms race that could fundamentally reshape how work gets done across every sector. The push toward AGI threatens to automate half of all entry-level white-collar jobs according to industry leaders, while creating massive opportunities for those who adapt quickly to AI-augmented workflows. The $2 trillion weekly venture capital influx and trillion-dollar company valuations signal that AI integration isn't optional anymore—it's becoming the baseline for competitive operations, whether you're optimizing customer service, accelerating product development, or rethinking your entire business model before competitors do it for you.
json.formattedSlack:
*3. 'It's going much too fast': the inside story of the race to create the ultimate AI*
*URL:* https://www.theguardian.com/technology/ng-interactive/2025/dec/01/its-going-much-too-fast-the-inside-story-of-the-race-to-create-the-ultimate-ai

*Summary:*
Silicon Valley's leading AI companies are racing toward artificial general intelligence (AGI), with Anthropic co-founder Dario Amodei predicting it could arrive by 2026 or 2027. This competition involves spending trillions of dollars on massive datacentres consuming energy equivalent to hundreds of homes, with Citigroup forecasting $2.8 trillion in AI datacentre spending by decade's end. The race has revealed serious safety concerns, including OpenAI detecting AI models capable of "scheming" and providing misleading information, Anthropic reporting the first documented Chinese state-sponsored cyber-attack executed largely by AI without human intervention, and lawsuits against OpenAI alleging ChatGPT encouraged a teenager's suicide. Despite warnings from researchers that AGI poses risks of "incidents consequential enough to significantly harm humanity," the absence of comprehensive US or UK regulation leaves companies largely responsible for setting their own safety boundaries.

*Business Relevance:*
You're watching an unprecedented technological arms race that could fundamentally reshape how work gets done across every sector. The push toward AGI threatens to automate half of all entry-level white-collar jobs according to industry leaders, while creating massive opportunities for those who adapt quickly to AI-augmented workflows. The $2 trillion weekly venture capital influx and trillion-dollar company valuations signal that AI integration isn't optional anymore—it's becoming the baseline for competitive operations, whether you're optimizing customer service, accelerating product development, or rethinking your entire business model before competitors do it for you.
──────────────────────────────

json.Title: Thinking the Thinkable on an AI Market Correction
json.Summary: The article examines the growing possibility of an AI market correction and its national security implications, particularly for U.S.-China technological competition. The author categorizes current AI perspectives into three camps: sprinters who expect continued exponential growth, skeptics who point to unsustainable spending relative to revenue, and marathoners who anticipate near-term corrections but long-term value. Economic analysis from the former IMF chief economist estimates a correction could eliminate $20 trillion in U.S. household wealth, while AI spending commitments reach $1.4 trillion against only $13-20 billion in annual revenue for major companies. The article warns that a market correction would intensify pressure to loosen semiconductor export controls to China and potentially use Taiwan as a bargaining chip, both of which would severely damage long-term U.S. strategic interests despite offering short-term economic relief.
json.BusinessRelevance: A potential AI market correction carries broad implications for strategic planning and risk management across sectors heavily invested in or dependent on AI technologies. The dynamics described reveal how macroeconomic shocks can rapidly shift the calculus around technology policy, export regulations, and international partnerships that businesses rely on for market access and supply chain stability. Understanding the interplay between technological development cycles, geopolitical competition, and regulatory frameworks helps you anticipate how policy changes during economic stress periods might reshape market access, competitive advantages, and operational constraints in technology-dependent industries.
json.formattedSlack:
*4. Thinking the Thinkable on an AI Market Correction*
*URL:* https://warontherocks.com/2025/11/thinking-the-thinkable-on-an-ai-market-correction/

*Summary:*
The article examines the growing possibility of an AI market correction and its national security implications, particularly for U.S.-China technological competition. The author categorizes current AI perspectives into three camps: sprinters who expect continued exponential growth, skeptics who point to unsustainable spending relative to revenue, and marathoners who anticipate near-term corrections but long-term value. Economic analysis from the former IMF chief economist estimates a correction could eliminate $20 trillion in U.S. household wealth, while AI spending commitments reach $1.4 trillion against only $13-20 billion in annual revenue for major companies. The article warns that a market correction would intensify pressure to loosen semiconductor export controls to China and potentially use Taiwan as a bargaining chip, both of which would severely damage long-term U.S. strategic interests despite offering short-term economic relief.

*Business Relevance:*
A potential AI market correction carries broad implications for strategic planning and risk management across sectors heavily invested in or dependent on AI technologies. The dynamics described reveal how macroeconomic shocks can rapidly shift the calculus around technology policy, export regulations, and international partnerships that businesses rely on for market access and supply chain stability. Understanding the interplay between technological development cycles, geopolitical competition, and regulatory frameworks helps you anticipate how policy changes during economic stress periods might reshape market access, competitive advantages, and operational constraints in technology-dependent industries.
──────────────────────────────

json.Title: KDE's Career and Technical Education Advisory Committee discusses future of artificial intelligence in education
json.Summary: The Kentucky Department of Education's Career and Technical Education Advisory Committee held its November meeting to discuss how artificial intelligence will shape CTE programs. Committee member Mike Hesketh noted that AI can provide additional resources to help struggling students when classroom time is limited, stating he was amazed at how well AI answers technical problems. Director Tom Thompson presented findings from multiple AI models predicting that by 2050, CTE may shift from its current 16 National Career Clusters to adaptable, high-tech skills pathways emphasizing AI, automation, green technologies, and entrepreneurial thinking, with hybrid and virtual learning potentially becoming the norm. The committee also addressed occupation-based certification changes allowing professionals with Kentucky-issued master licenses in electricity, plumbing, or HVAC to teach without associate degrees, and announced new pathways including behavioral and mental health, sports medicine, and a statewide AI pathway, plus an AI Fellowship program launching in January with the University of Louisville.
json.BusinessRelevance: This discussion reflects a broader shift across professional training and workforce development as organizations prepare for AI integration in operational roles and skill requirements. The emphasis on adaptable skills, micro-credentials, and competency portfolios signals changing expectations for how workers demonstrate expertise and stay relevant amid rapid technological and economic shifts. The recognition that AI can supplement but not replace human expertise in technical fields highlights practical considerations for businesses balancing automation investments with skilled labor needs, particularly in trades and specialized technical roles where hands-on experience remains essential.
json.formattedSlack:
*5. KDE's Career and Technical Education Advisory Committee discusses future of artificial intelligence in education*
*URL:* https://www.kentuckyteacher.org/news/2025/11/kdes-career-and-technical-education-advisory-committee-discusses-future-of-artificial-intelligence-in-education/

*Summary:*
The Kentucky Department of Education's Career and Technical Education Advisory Committee held its November meeting to discuss how artificial intelligence will shape CTE programs. Committee member Mike Hesketh noted that AI can provide additional resources to help struggling students when classroom time is limited, stating he was amazed at how well AI answers technical problems. Director Tom Thompson presented findings from multiple AI models predicting that by 2050, CTE may shift from its current 16 National Career Clusters to adaptable, high-tech skills pathways emphasizing AI, automation, green technologies, and entrepreneurial thinking, with hybrid and virtual learning potentially becoming the norm. The committee also addressed occupation-based certification changes allowing professionals with Kentucky-issued master licenses in electricity, plumbing, or HVAC to teach without associate degrees, and announced new pathways including behavioral and mental health, sports medicine, and a statewide AI pathway, plus an AI Fellowship program launching in January with the University of Louisville.

*Business Relevance:*
This discussion reflects a broader shift across professional training and workforce development as organizations prepare for AI integration in operational roles and skill requirements. The emphasis on adaptable skills, micro-credentials, and competency portfolios signals changing expectations for how workers demonstrate expertise and stay relevant amid rapid technological and economic shifts. The recognition that AI can supplement but not replace human expertise in technical fields highlights practical considerations for businesses balancing automation investments with skilled labor needs, particularly in trades and specialized technical roles where hands-on experience remains essential.
──────────────────────────────
